edges.description: Infected tonsillar T cells enter the bloodstream, producing cell-associated viremia.
edges.source: Primary VZV infection via respiratory mucosa

edges.description: Viremia seeds VZV to skin where glycoprotein-mediated cell-to-cell spread produces vesicular lesions.
During viremia, VZV also gains access to peripheral nerves and sensory ganglia.
edges.target: Establishment of latency in sensory ganglia

nodes.description: VZV infects epidermal keratinocytes, causing ballooning degeneration, multinucleated giant cell formation, and intraepidermal vesiculation. Viral envelope glycoproteins (gB/gE, gH/gL) promote cell-to-cell fusion and syncytium formation, supporting spread through skin without requiring extracellular virion release. The characteristic rash progresses through macules, papules, vesicles, and crusts, with lesions in multiple stages simultaneously (centripetal distribution).

evidence.reference_title: Varicella Zoster Virus disrupts MAIT cell polyfunctional effector responses.
evidence.snippet: we report a virus-mediated immune-evasion strategy that disarms MAIT cell responses.

evidence.reference_title: Absence of intercellular adhesion molecule 1 expression in varicella zoster virus-infected keratinocytes during herpes zoster: another immune evasion strategy?
evidence.snippet: HLA-DR and ICAM-1 expressions were not observed in VZV- and HSV-infected keratinocytes, contrasting with their upregulation in the surrounding epidermis and inside nonviral blisters.

evidence.reference_title: Varicella zoster virus glycoprotein E facilitates PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy to evade STING and MAVS-mediated antiviral innate immunity.
evidence.snippet: VZV gE inhibited MAVS oligomerization and STING translocation to disrupt MAVS- and STING-mediated interferon (IFN) responses, and PINK1/Parkin-mediated mitophagy was required for VZV gE-mediated inhibition of IFN production.

nodes.description: During primary infection, VZV gains access to peripheral nerves and sensory ganglia, establishing lifelong latency in cranial nerve and dorsal root ganglia neurons. Latency is characterized by highly restricted viral transcription dominated by VZV latency-associated transcripts (VLTs) and VLT-ORF63 fusion transcripts, while most lytic genes are silenced. The virus may reactivate decades later as herpes zoster, particularly with aging or immunosuppression.
nodes.id: Establishment of latency in sensory ganglia

cell_types: Sensory neuron of dorsal root ganglion

evidence.reference_title: Identification and characterization of Varicella Zoster Virus circular RNA in lytic infection.
evidence.snippet: we discover numerous VZV latency-associated transcripts (VLTs)-like circRNAs (circVLTslytic), which contain multiple exons and different isoforms within the same back-splicing breakpoint.

evidence.reference_title: The Spectrum of Neurological Manifestations of Varicella-Zoster Virus Reactivation.
evidence.snippet: following a variable period of latency in different ganglionic neurons, it reactivates to produce herpes zoster (shingles).

evidence.reference_title: Varicella-zoster virus human ganglionic latency: a current summary.
evidence.snippet: It is now established beyond doubt that latent VZV is predominantly located in human ganglionic neurons. Virus gene transcription during latency is epigenetically regulated, and appears to be restricted to expression of at least six genes, with expression of gene 63 being the hallmark of latency.

nodes.description: VZV enters the host through the upper respiratory tract via inhalation of infectious aerosols or direct contact with vesicle fluid. The virus initially replicates in the nasopharyngeal lymphoid tissue (including tonsils) and regional lymph nodes, infecting CD4+ T lymphocytes with activation/memory and skin-homing features that serve as vehicles for subsequent systemic dissemination.
nodes.id: Primary VZV infection via respiratory mucosa

evidence.reference_title: The Spectrum of Neurological Manifestations of Varicella-Zoster Virus Reactivation.
evidence.snippet: Varicella-Zoster virus (VZV) is a pathogenic human alpha herpes virus that causes varicella (chicken pox) as a primary infection and, following a variable period of latency in different ganglionic neurons, it reactivates to produce herpes zoster (shingles).

evidence.reference_title: T-cell tropism and the role of ORF66 protein in pathogenesis of varicella-zoster virus infection.
evidence.snippet: The pathogenesis of varicella-zoster virus (VZV) involves a cell-associated viremia during which infectious virus is carried from sites of respiratory mucosal inoculation to the skin.

nodes.description: Following initial replication in lymphoid tissue, VZV disseminates via cell-associated viremia in T lymphocytes. VZV DNA is detectable in T-lymphocytes as early as 8-10 days prior to rash onset. The secondary viremia seeds the virus to the skin and mucous membranes, producing the characteristic vesicular exanthem. Concurrent innate immune evasion through IRF3 antagonism (by ORF61p, ORF47p, and IE-62) and suppression of type I interferon signaling facilitates systemic spread.

evidence.reference_title: Varicella-zoster virus and virus DNA in the blood and oropharynx of people with latent or active varicella-zoster virus infections.
evidence.snippet: VZV DNA, primarily in T-lymphocytes, is detected as early as 8-10 days prior to rash and can persist for a week.

evidence.reference_title: Suppression of the host antiviral response by non-infectious varicella zoster virus extracellular vesicles.
evidence.snippet: VZV sEVs are non-infectious yet transcriptionally altered primary human cells, suppressing the antiviral type 1 interferon response and promoting neuroinvasion of a secondary pathogen in vivo.

evidence.reference_title: Varicella-zoster virus immediate-early protein ORF61 abrogates the IRF3-mediated innate immune response through degradation of activated IRF3.
evidence.snippet: ORF61 down-modulates the IRF3-mediated IFN-β pathway by degradation of activated IRF3 via direct interaction, which may contribute to the pathogenesis of VZV infection.

evidence.reference_title: T-Cell tropism of simian varicella virus during primary infection.
evidence.snippet: During viremia the virus preferentially infected memory T-cells, initially central memory T-cells and subsequently effector memory T-cells.
